title: Biases usually fire before arguments become polished
summary: Logical fallacies often show up in public-facing argument structure. Cognitive biases often distort what feels plausible even earlier, in private judgment, attention, memory, and interpretation.
paragraphs: That difference matters because people often arrive at the argument already bent toward one interpretation. By the time a clean verbal fallacy appears, attention, retrieval, emotional salience, and identity-protective filtering may have done most of the work.

A bias site therefore needs different teaching primitives. The useful questions are not only 'What argument form failed?' but also 'What felt easy, normal, vivid, or self-protective before the person ever stated a conclusion out loud?'
This is why CogBias focuses on distorted judgment, cross-situational comparison, and procedural repair rather than on public argument structure alone.
bullets: Fallacies are often easiest to observe in public discourse.
Biases often explain how the discourse got tilted before the public exchange began.
Debiasing therefore requires process changes, not just better labels.

title: Why CogBias uses both categories and patterns
summary: One taxonomy groups biases by the kind of judgment that is failing. The other groups them by the shape of the distortion that keeps recurring across contexts.
paragraphs: The category layer answers a practical diagnostic question: is this mainly a failure of estimation, decision, evidence assessment, causal attribution, memory, or reported opinion? That is often the fastest way to narrow the field.

The pattern layer answers a different question: what kind of pull is doing the hidden work? Is the judgment being bent by vivid association, by an inherited baseline, by inertia, by the known outcome, or by self-protective perspective?
Using both layers makes the site more teachable. It helps readers see why two biases can live far apart alphabetically but still feel psychologically similar in use.
bullets: Categories are about the task being distorted.
Patterns are about the mechanism of distortion.
Together they make comparison easier than a flat alphabetical list.

title: Debiasing works better as process design than as moral exhortation
summary: Knowing the label is useful, but recognition alone rarely fixes the bias. Good debiasing usually changes the decision environment itself.
paragraphs: People often imagine that once a bias is named, it can simply be willed away. In practice, many distortions are sticky precisely because they feel like ordinary good judgment from the inside. Awareness helps, but structure helps more.

That is why CogBias emphasizes countermoves, self-audits, and path-based study. The practical question is not whether someone can recite a definition. It is whether the workflow now contains a pause, comparison, reference class, rubric, or postmortem habit that interrupts the old drift.

At the individual level this may look like a written forecast, a reference-class check, or a rival-hypothesis note. At the team level it may look like independent scoring, premortems, or explicit role assignment. At the system level it means defaults, dashboards, and review rules that make the bias harder to reproduce.
bullets: Recognition is necessary but rarely sufficient.
The most reliable repair is often structural, not merely motivational.
A good teaching site should therefore help readers change procedure, not just vocabulary.

title: Familiarity, fluency, and understanding are not the same thing
summary: Many bias problems begin when a person mistakes recognition, smooth explanation, or comfortable vocabulary for real depth of understanding.
paragraphs: This matters for both learning and leadership. A person can sound increasingly competent as the language becomes more familiar while still lacking the ability to explain the mechanism, spot edge cases, or perform the task under pressure.

That is why CogBias gives serious space to overconfidence, the Dunning-Kruger effect, the illusion of explanatory depth, and the curse of knowledge. Together they explain why confidence and comprehension drift apart so easily.
A strong teaching site should therefore help readers test understanding, not merely rehearse labels. Good prompts ask for mechanism, contrast cases, challenge questions, and task-level demonstration.
bullets: Recognition is weaker than explanation.
Explanation is weaker than performance.
Fluency without feedback is one of the easiest routes to false confidence.

title: Groups distort judgment before they distort the minutes
summary: In group settings, social cost can bend what people notice, what they report, and what they are willing to challenge long before a formal decision is recorded.
paragraphs: One reason groupthink is so dangerous is that it does not only affect the final vote. It changes who speaks, how objections are phrased, how much friction a dominant view faces, and even what later feels like the room's genuine consensus.

Authority bias, social desirability bias, false-consensus effect, and ingroup pressure often work together. The visible decision can therefore look tidy even while private judgment inside the group is far more uneven.

This is why CogBias leans on independent first passes, dissent rounds, and meeting protocols. If the social environment is doing hidden cognitive work, the repair has to change the room, not just remind people to be brave.
bullets: Consensus quality depends on the cost of dissent.
The meeting process is part of the cognitive environment.
Silence is often socially produced before it is evidentially meaningful.

title: The default is not neutral just because it is quiet
summary: Defaults, omissions, and inherited arrangements can carry as much decision weight as active choices, even when they feel administratively invisible.
paragraphs: People often reserve moral and analytical scrutiny for active intervention while treating defaults as background. But in practice, a default can encode priorities, shape incentives, and steer outcomes at scale.

That is why loss aversion, omission bias, default effect, and status quo bias belong together pedagogically. They explain how the mind overprotects what is already selected, already owned, or already underway.

A practical bias site should help readers expose the hidden choice architecture around them. The question is not only 'What did I choose?' but also 'What quietly chose for me because it arrived preloaded?'
bullets: The absence of action is not the absence of causation.
Preselection often smuggles in endorsement.
Good decision hygiene compares the default against the alternatives explicitly.

title: Good teaching separates confidence from competence
summary: A useful site does not merely warn against overconfidence. It shows how to tell calibrated understanding, shallow fluency, and social confidence apart.
paragraphs: That distinction matters because people often reach for confidence as a practical proxy. In classrooms, interviews, meetings, and public discourse, certainty is easier to observe than actual reasoning depth. But it is often a poor stand-in.

CogBias therefore tries to teach with comparison tools instead of just slogans. Readers need concrete checks: explain it plainly, compare it to a rival case, recover the base rate, or state what would falsify the current view.
When the site works well, the user leaves with a better diagnostic habit, not just a sharper vocabulary. That is the teaching ambition carried through the whole project.
bullets: Confidence is observable, but observability is not validity.
Competence usually reveals itself under comparison, constraint, or challenge.
Teaching improves when the test for understanding is built into the workflow.
